coredata.dc:description: We deposited the highly transparent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) incorporated ZnO (N-CQDs/ZnO) hybrid thin films by sol–gel spin-coating process. TEM studies show a narrow size distribution of hydrothermally synthesized N-CQDs in the range of 2.5 to 3 nm. The SEM images ascertain the formation of ZnO nanoparticles upon incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid films. Raman studies show the hexagonal wurtzite structure of spin-coated ZnO thin film and ascertain the presence of incorporated N-CQDs adsorbed on the surface of ZnO in the hybrid films. The incorporated N-CQDs get adsorbed onto the surface of ZnO crystallites and control its size at nanoscale in the hybrid thin films. The XPS study also confirms the incorporation of nitrogen-doped CQDs and the existence of chemical interaction (chemisorption) between N-CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles, making the pathways for favorable energy transfer between N-CQDs and ZnO in the hybrid thin films. The band-edge emission (383 nm) of ZnO film increases for the increasing concentration of N-CQDs upon UV excitation (325 nm), showing the non-radiative UV energy transfer from N-CQDs to ZnO in the hybrid thin films along with the contribution of photoluminescence emission of N-CQDs in the visible region.

head.ce:title: Solution processed highly transparent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/ZnO hybrid thin films: A study on structural and enhanced UV emission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation of the formation of N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films, the observed visible emission of N-CQDs, and enhanced UV band-edge emission of ZnO at λex = 325 nm.

We deposited the highly transparent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) incorporated ZnO (N-CQDs/ZnO) hybrid thin films by sol–gel spin-coating process. TEM studies show a narrow size distribution of hydrothermally synthesized N-CQDs in the range of 2.5 to 3 nm. The SEM images ascertain the formation of ZnO nanoparticles upon incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid films. Raman studies show the hexagonal wurtzite structure of spin-coated ZnO thin film and ascertain the presence of incorporated N-CQDs adsorbed on the surface of ZnO in the hybrid films. The incorporated N-CQDs get adsorbed onto the surface of ZnO crystallites and control its size at nanoscale in the hybrid thin films. The XPS study also confirms the incorporation of nitrogen-doped CQDs and the existence of chemical interaction (chemisorption) between N-CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles, making the pathways for favorable energy transfer between N-CQDs and ZnO in the hybrid thin films. The band-edge emission (383 nm) of ZnO film increases for the increasing concentration of N-CQDs upon UV excitation (325 nm), showing the non-radiative UV energy transfer from N-CQDs to ZnO in the hybrid thin films along with the contribution of photoluminescence emission of N-CQDs in the visible region.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The working familiarity of nano-dimensional semiconducting material(s) in which the constituent components are built in atomic/molecular level, engineering their (structural, optical, and electrical) properties, device design at the nanoscale, achieving higher device performances, and necessary packing process (the integration of nanoscale devices) are considered to be the most essential things in order to realize miniaturized device modules for the future electronic and photonic technologies [1–3]. In the era of revolutionary semiconductor devices, zinc oxide (ZnO) was initially being used as a substrate material due to its close lattice-match for the growth of gallium nitride and related alloy(s) thin films [4,5]. Because of the ability to grow bulk single crystals or highly crystalline thin films and able to control their electrical conductivity, ZnO has, as a matter of extensive debate and research, attracted enormous research attention world-wide in the recent decades for their potential applications in the next generation semiconductor device technologies. As it is a very promising n-type metal oxide semiconductor material having a direct wide energy band gap (∼3.4 eV), a large free-exciton binding energy (∼60 meV) to have excitonic emission processes even at slightly elevated temperature, wide transmittance in the visible region, and high mechanical stability, ZnO has been utilized in the wide range of devices such as light emitting diodes, photodiodes, solar cells, photodetectors, Ultraviolet (UV) lasers, transparent thin film transistors, piezo-electric devices, etc. [4–13]. In addition to the various deposition methods such as atomic layer deposition, pulsed laser deposition, molecular beam epitaxy, metal–organic chemical vapor deposition, and chemical vapor transport technique for the preparation of high quality ZnO thin films, the sol–gel derived spin-coated ZnO thin films exhibit higher transparency and good crystalline properties, which are very much useful for the fabrication of highly efficient cost-effective solution processed optoelectronic devices [9–15]. The vacuum sublimated conjugated organic semiconductor thin films have recently been utilized in various optoelectronic devices [organic light emitting diodes (OLEDs), organic solar cells (OPVs), organic thin film transistors (OTFTs), etc.] due to their good optical and electrical characteristics, non-toxic and environmental friendly (biodegradable), and high luminance efficiency in the wide color gamut [16–22]. The organic/inorganic hybrid composites exhibit extremely promising characteristics by combing the individual properties of organic and inorganic materials with the effective electronic interaction and efficient charge transfer at organic/inorganic interface at the nanoscale [23,24]. In our recent report, tris-(8-hydroxyquinoline)aluminum (Alq3), one of the most widely used electron transporting as well as light emitting materials in the organic light emitting diodes, incorporated ZnO (Alq3/ZnO) hybrid thin films were deposited by sol–gel spin coating process, exhibiting a four-fold enhancement in the UV band edge emission of ZnO nanoparticles in the hybrid film by efficient energy transfer (non-radiative) from Alq3 to ZnO [14,15]. The spin-coated Alq3/ZnO hybrid thin films have showed the potential applications in the future solution processed optoelectronic devices.

In the recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (emerged as a new class of eco-friendly nanoparticles having ultrafine particle size of below 10 nm) have ignited enormous research interest worldwide due to their remarkable advantages such as high luminescence, extreme photostability, non-toxicity, cost effective synthesis, and excellent water solubility when compared to conventional inorganic semiconductors and fluorescent organic semiconducting molecules [25–27]. The CQDs having carbon core with abundant surface functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups (providing excellent water solubility and suitable chemical reaction) exhibit tunable electronic band gap due to quantum confinement effect, strong light absorption, stable photoluminescence (PL) from deep UV to near IR, efficient multiphoton up-conversion efficiency, high electron mobility, and long hot-electron lifetimes, upon by manipulating their particle size, shape, and chemical doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulphur, boron, phosphorous [27]. The CQDs have shown their potential applications in various photonic and optoelectronic devices such as OLEDs, OPVs, photodetectors, lasers, optical sensors, bioimaging, and photo catalyst [28–32]. The incorporation of CQDs into the inorganic semiconductor matrix maximizes the interaction of these functionalized nanostructures with the inorganic environment in the organic–inorganic hybrid nanocomposites, resulting in the improved performances of aforementioned technologically advanced devices. Suzuki et al. has prepared the ZnO porous films containing CQDs through a one-pot synthesis and showed the wavelength tuned photoluminescence in the visible region, attributed to the energy transfer from ZnO (having band-edge emission in the UV region and broad defect emission in the visible region) to CQDs (a wide emission in the visible region) due to dipole interactions and reabsorption between them [33]. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-sensitized ZnO nanorod based UV photodetector demonstrated higher photocurrent with eight times higher external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 13161 % at −1 V (340 nm, 80 mW/cm2) when compared to untreated ZnO nanorod based device (pristine), attributed to the charge carrier injection by induced surface traps at the GQDs/ZnO nanorods (NRs) [34]. Kathalingam et al. has also reported the preparation of GQDs-wrapped vertically aligned hydrothermally grown ZnO NRs arrays on ITO substrate as ZnO NR/GQDs (core/shell) heterostructure by a facile approach of combining spin-coating, electron-beam evaporation, and a rapid thermal process in a novel way. This heterostructure based devices (ITO/ZnO(NR)/GQDs/Ni or In) exhibited improved UV photo sensing properties with excellent photo response due to the higher charge carrier transport and light absorption characteristics of GQDs [35]. Chava et al. has used the hydrothermal synthesized nitrogen (N)-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) to sensitize the ZnO nanoparticles based photoanode and reported the higher photocurrent due to significantly enhanced visible light absorption and effective charge separation by anchored N-CQDs [36]. The various reports essentially show that the CQDs and/or heteroatoms such as nitrogen-doped CQDs with improved PL quantum efficiency (PLQE) demonstrate the enhanced charge injection and/or efficient charge separation at CQDs/ZnO nanoscale heterojunction interface in the CQDs-ZnO complex composites, showing their promising applications in highly efficient solution processed optoelectronic devices particularly in the UV photodetectors [37–39].

In this study, the nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterized. The effects of incorporation of N-CQDs on the structural, morphological, and optical properties of ZnO in the sol–gel derived spin coated N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films were analyzed using various spectroscopic techniques for their potential applications in the solution-processed optoelectronic devices.

Synthesis of nitrogen- doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs): The N-CQDs were synthesized by a single step hydrothermal method using the widely-used procedure reported elsewhere [34,36,40]. The citric acid (C6H8O7: 99 %; Merck, India) as source of organic carbon and urea (CH4N2O: 99 %; Merck, India) as nitrogen source in the concentration ratio of (1:3) (mM) were dissolved in 100 mL of double-ionized (DI) water with rigorous stirring for 10 min. The obtained transparent solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (volume: 150 mL). The sealed autoclave was heated at 190 °C for 8 h in the vacuum oven and allowed to cool down naturally to room temperature. The N-CQDs product was separated from the reacted brown-black aqueous solution by filtering it using 0.22 µm syringe filter membrane and then vacuum dried at 130 °C for 12 h to completely evaporate the solvent used. The obtained N-CQDs was thoroughly cleaned several times with ethanol and vacuum dried at each step. The synthesized pure N-CQDs powder was re-dispersed into DI water for further characterization studies and to be used in thin film preparation.

Synthesis of ZnO sol–gel precursor: The preparation of ZnO sol–gel precursor was followed from our earlier report published elsewhere [14]. The 1.646 g (0.5 M) of zinc acetate dihydrate [Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O; Sigma Aldrich (98.5 %)] was dissolved into 15 mL of 2-methoxyethanol (C3H8O2: 99 %; SRL) solvent (10.97 wt to volume percentage [%(w/v)] of zinc acetate) and the solution was stirred (450 rpm for 2 hrs) at 65 °C with a drop-wise addition (0.453 mL) of monoethanolamine (MEA: 99 %; SRL) stabilizer for 1:1 M ratio of MEA to Zinc acetate. Then, this solution was allowed for gelation under the normal ambient for 24 hrs. The obtained ZnO sol–gel precursor (solution A) was filtered using 0.22 µm syringe filter for the preparation of pristine ZnO thin films (film a).

Preparation of N-CQDs incorporated ZnO sol–gel precursor: Initially, the 0.4 mg (for solution B) or 0.8 mg (for solution C) of N-CQDs powder was dispersed into a solvent mixer of 2-methoxyethanol (3 mL) and MEA stabilizer (3 µL) and then it was sonicated for 30 min followed by rigorous stirring for 16 hrs at 65 °C to obtain the uniform dispersion of N-CQDs. The solution B had the concentration of 13.33 x10-3 %(w/v) of N-CQDs and the solution C had 26.66 x10-3 %(w/v) of N-CQDs. The 1 mL of solution B was added with the 2 mL of solution A to obtain the precursor having the molar concentration of 0.33 M or 7.32 %(w/v) of Zinc acetate and 4.4 × 10-3 %(w/v) of N-CQDs in 3 mL. The obtained N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid sol–gel precursor (3 mL) had 0.6 x10-3; the nominal ratio of N-CQDs/ZnO [%(w/v)] (for film b). The 1 mL of solution C was added with the 2 mL of solution A to obtain the N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid sol–gel precursor (3 mL) (for film c) having the molar concentration of 0.33 M or 7.32 %(w/v) of Zinc acetate and 8.8 × 10-3 %(w/v) of N-CQDs in 3 mL, resulting in 1.2 x10-3; the nominal ratio of N-CQDs/ZnO [%(w/v)] as given in the Table 1 .

The glass substrates were thoroughly cleaned prior to the coating using the procedure reported elsewhere in order to remove the organic solvents and other contaminations on the surface [14]. First ultrasonically cleaned in acetone followed by the isopropyl alcohol (isopropanol) (IPA) and DI water for 10 min each. The substrates were dried in vacuum oven at 100 °C for about 10 min in each step. The oxide layer formed on the substrates during the exposure under the normal ambient was etched out using high power plasma cleaner (Harrick Plasma, PDC-002). The filtered ZnO sol–gel precursor (solution A) was used to deposit the pristine ZnO thin film (a) using the Spin coater (Apex Instruments, spinNXG-P2 model) under the normal ambient. The N-CQDs incorporated ZnO (N-CQDs/ZnO) hybrid thin films with the nominal ratio (0.6 x10-3 for the film b and 1.2 x10-3 for the film c) of N-CQDs/ZnO [%(w/v)], were spin-coated at 2000 rpm for 30 s. The spin-coating was repeated for 5 times for each film (a - c) to obtain the optimal thickness of around 200 nm and dried at 180 °C for 10 min on a hot plate with glass-lid covering under the normal ambient after each coating. In order to improve the film quality and evaporate the residue solvents, the sol–gel derived spin-coated pristine ZnO (a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b - c) were then post-annealed at 350 °C for 2 hrs in a tubular furnace under the normal ambient.

The UV–visible transmission spectra of the solution-processed pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) were measured using UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Agilent Carry 5000). The PL spectra of the spin-coated thin films (a - c) were recorded using Horiba Jobin-Yvon, Fluorolog3. The surface morphology and elemental analysis were performed using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (TESCAN, VEGA3 LMU model) integrated with the energy dispersive analysis using X-rays (EDAX) (Oxford Instruments, INCAx-act model). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of hydrothermal synthesized N-CQDs powder sample were taken from the TEM system (ThermoFisher Scientific, Talos F200 S model). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected from X-ray diffractometer (PANanalytical Netherlands, XPERT-PRO model) having X-ray source (Cu K-alpha radiation; 1.5406 Å) operated at 45 kV and 30 mA. The diffraction spectra were analyzed using X'Pert Highscore Plus software. The FT-IR spectra of the hybrid thin films (a - c) were recorded using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (JASCO Co. Japan, FT/IR-6600 model). The elemental compositions of sol–gel derived spin-coated thin films (a - c) were evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Physical Electronics, PHI 5802 model) using a monochromatized X-ray source (Al Kα radiation; hν = 1486.6 eV). The binding energy (BE) of C 1 s peak (283.5 eV) of pristine ZnO thin film (a) was used as a charge reference and the charge correction (energy-scale calibration) for all core level spectra of hybrid thin films (b and c) was carried out with reference to C 1 s peak at 283.5 eV. The Raman spectra of the pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) were obtained from Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France, LabRAM HR model) with a Helium Neon laser (excitation: 633 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films
ce:section.ce:para:
The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and incorporated in the ZnO thin films. The pristine ZnO (film a) with the concentration of 0.5 M [10.97 %(w/v)] and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films with the nominal ratio (0.6 x10-3 for film b and 1.2 x10-3 for film c) of N-CQDs/ZnO [%(w/v)], were deposited on the clean glass substrates by using spin-coating method. Fig. 1 (i) shows the absorption spectrum of hydrothermally synthesized N-CQDs, exhibiting a characteristic broad absorption peaking at around 333 nm (corresponding to n-π* transitions of CN and CO groups) along with a tail absorption extending into the visible region due to the surface states related to the functional surface groups (which form low-energy sub-band gaps within the n-π* band gap) connected at the edges of the synthesized N-CQDs [41–43]. The characteristic absorption below 288 nm normally attributed to aromatic carbon sp2 hybridized core domains (π-π* and σ-π* transitions) is not covered in our measurements. The optical band gap of the N-CQDs is determined to be 2.72 eV using the Tauc relation. The physical appearance of N-CQDs dispersed solution exhibited the size-dependent blue fluorescence under the exposure to UV light of 365 nm when compared to the day light exposure [inset of Fig. 1(i)] [36]. The UV–visible transmittance spectra of sol–gel derived spin-coated pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) were shown in the Fig. 1(ii). The pristine ZnO thin film exhibited the higher characteristic transmittance of 90 % at 550 nm with the average transmittance of 85 %. It is observed that the transmittance of N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films is slightly decreased [88 % (84 %; average transmittance) for film (b) and 84 % (81 %; average transmittance) for film (c) at 550 nm] upon the increasing content of N-CQDs, attributed to the fact that the incorporated N-CQDs get adsorbed at the grain boundaries of ZnO and act as light absorbing and scattering centers in the hybrid thin films [14,15,44]. A slight shift towards lower wavelength (blue-shift) in the UV absorption edge of the N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films when compared to that of pristine ZnO film reveals the effect of confinement of electrons (quantum confinement effect) and quantization of energy states in the ZnO for increasing N-CQDs content [45]. The optical band gap (Eg) of the sol–gel derived spin-coated pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) is determined by using the following Tauc formula
α h υ

Where hυ is the energy of the incident photon, A is the constant, and α is the optical absorption coefficient of the film. The inset of Fig. 1(ii) shows the plot of (αhυ)2 as a function of energy of the photon (hυ). The optical band gap (Eg) of thin films can be obtained by extrapolating the linear portion of (αhυ)2 to the point where α = 0 on the plot of photon energy (hυ). The solution processed pristine ZnO thin film exhibited the direct optical band gap energy (Eg) of 3.27 eV in consistency with the earlier reports [44,46–48]. The slight increase in the band gap energy of hybrid thin films (3.28 eV for film c) and significant decrease in the absorbance in the UV region ascertain the quantum confinement of ZnO and the incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid thin films [49,50].

The XRD pattern of N-CQDs powder (Fig. 2 (i)) shows a broad peak centered at around 25° corresponding to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) having highly polycrystalline (amorphous) core structure [40]. This observed broad diffraction pattern is also similar to the reported graphite lattice spacing with lattice contraction (commonly observed for the nanomaterials in nanoscale regime), attributed to rich active sites on the surface of hydrothermally prepared CQDs [51,52]. It reveals that the hydrothermally synthesized N-CQDs are polycrystalline with rich active sites at the edges. The XRD patterns of the pristine ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (a - c) are shown in Fig. 2(i). The spin-coated pristine ZnO film (a) exhibits good crystalline properties with hexagonal wurtzite structure by the diffraction peaks at the angles (2θ) (assigned ‘hkl’ plane) of 32.09° (100), 34.70° (002), 36.59° (101), 47.86° (102), 56.95° (110), and 63.12° (103) (JCPDS file: 96–101-1259) [14,50]. No change in the diffraction peak’s position is observed but the decreasing intensity of diffraction peaks upon increasing N-CQDs content in the N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c), supporting the fact that the incorporated N-CQDs do not enter into the ZnO matrix and may get adsorbed onto the grain boundaries of ZnO [14]. The texture coefficient (TChkl) values of the films (a - c) are estimated using the given formula and shown in the Fig. 2(ii).

Where Ihkl is the measured intensity of the diffraction peak of assigned hkl plane, I0hkl is the standard peak intensity of the hkl plane from JCPDS file, and N is the total number of diffraction peaks observed in the XRD pattern of the respective film. The pristine ZnO (film a) exhibited the higher textured orientation along (002) plane (2θ: 31.80 °), attributed to enhanced growth along the (002) plane due to higher induced-micro strain in the film [53]. It is observed that the increasing N-CQDs content suppresses the preferred textured growth along the (002) plane in the hybrid thin films. This attributes the fact that the incorporated N-CQDs may preferably be adsorbed onto the surface of ZnO crystallites having the higher surface energy along the (002) orientation [54]. The average crystallite size of pristine ZnO (a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) is estimated using Debye-Scherrer’s formula
Crystallite size
D
=
k λ / β
cos
θ

Where X-ray wavelength (λ) is 1.5406 Å, the correction factor (k) is 0.94, and the full width at half maxima (FWHM) of the diffraction peak at the angle (θ) is β. The presence of incorporated N-CQDs onto the surface of ZnO may have caused the slight increase in the crystallite size of the hybrid thin film (b) (Fig. 2(iii)). Further increasing of N-CQDs content decreases ZnO crystallite size in the hybrid thin film (c), revealing that the incorporated N-CQDs may preferably get adsorbed at the higher surface energy sites of ZnO crystallites along the (002) plane orientation and control its size in nanoscale [14].

The HR-TEM images in the Fig. 3 (i) reveal that the hydrothermally synthesized N-CQDs are homogeneously dispersed in aqueous solution and having relatively uniform particle size distribution without any apparent aggregation. The obtained N-CQDs are almost spherical shaped with an average particle size distribution of 2.5–3 nm (Fig. 3(ii)) with the lattice fringe distance (d) of 0.34 nm, which is attributed to (100) diffraction plane of graphite structure [55]. The EDAX analysis [Fig. 3(iii)] shows the prepared N-CQDs have carbon (46 %), nitrogen (17 %), and oxygen (37 %). The surface morphological features of the pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) are obtained using SEM studies and shown in Fig. 3(iv-vi). From the Fig. 3(iv), it is found that the sol–gel derived spin-coated pristine ZnO thin film exhibited root-like wrinkled surface morphology, which is commonly observed morphological features of the solution processed ZnO thin films [14,50,56]. This is attributed to the fact that upon annealing of the film the substantial loss of precursor solvent by evaporation causes a large amount of stress, leading not only to the bending of the film but also to the contraction of the skeletal network and resulting in such a wrinkled surface morphological features of the solution processed ZnO thin films [50]. It is also observed that the incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid thin film (b) induces particle-like structure along with reduced thickness of root-like wrinkles [Fig. 3(v)]. The significant decrease in the size of the observed particles is distinctly visible upon further increase of N-CQDs content in the hybrid thin film (c) [Fig. 3(vi)]. The observed particle structure in N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films supports the XRD results that incorporated N-CQDs get adsorbed at the grain boundaries of ZnO crystallites and cap the ZnO particles to control its size in nanoscale. The similar effect has been observed when organic molecules are incorporated in organic/ZnO hybrid thin films as reported elsewhere [14].

The EDAX spectra and elemental mapping of the constituent elements of pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) are shown in Fig. 4 . The pristine ZnO thin film (a) exhibited the atomic concentrations (atomic percentage; at.%) of the main constituent elements Zn and O along with the Si atoms originated from the glass substrate and presence of C atoms arising from handing the film under the normal ambient. The uniform distribution of major constituent elements (Zn and O) throughout the entire region of the film (a) is evident from EDAX elemental mapping analysis. The ratio of atomic percentage of C atoms (major constituent element of N-CQDs) to Zn atoms (C/Zn) is considered to be a measure to quantify the incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid thin films (b and c). The N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin film (b) showed a significant increase in the atomic percentage ratio of C/Zn (2.71) when compared to that (C/Zn = 1.03) of pristine film (a), which is also supported by relatively increased density of constituent O and C atoms with uniform distributions of elements in the elemental mapping of hybrid film (b). These results reveal the presence of incorporated N-CQDs on the surface of hybrid thin film (b). Further increase of N-CQDs content in the hybrid thin film (c) also exhibited the uniform distribution of constituent elements (C, Zn and O atoms) over the entire region with slightly decreased ratio of C/Zn (1.45) when compared to the hybrid thin film (b), showing the incorporated N-CQDs entering into the bulk of the hybrid film in support of the fact that the incorporated N-CQDs may get adsorbed onto the surface of ZnO crystallites as protector to control its size in nanoscale [14,15].

The chemical composition and their structural changes of the N-CQDs powder, pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) were studied using the FTIR spectra given in Fig. 5 . The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 5(i)) of the hydrothermally synthesized N-CQDs powder reveals the significant absorption bands peaked at 3445 cm−1 (OH), 2924 cm−1 (CH), 1659 cm−1 (CO), 1384 cm−1 (CC), and 1090 cm−1 (CO), ascribed to the characteristic stretching vibrations of various functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl) of the obtained N-CQDs sample [57–59]. The observed CC stretching mode ascertains the presence of sp2 hybridized graphitic lattice structure for π-electron transitions in the core of N-CQDs and the various functional groups indicate that the -OH, -CO, -CO, and -CH surface moieties were decorated at the edges and onto the basal-plane of the N-CQDs [57]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 5(ii)) of the pristine ZnO thin film (a) shows a broad characteristic strong absorption band (400–600 cm−1) centered at 404 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of Zn-O [14,60]. The IR absorption band peaked at 911 cm−1 (assigned to CH bending vibrational mode) along with the weak absorption bands appeared at around 1594/2191 cm−1 (CC stretching), 1739 cm−1 (CO stretching), 2923 cm−1 (CH stretching), and 3421 cm−1 (OH stretching), are commonly observed in the solution processed ZnO thin films due to the residue formed from the precursors and coating ambient [14,60]. The N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) also exhibited all the characteristic IR absorption bands (without any significant change in the shape and peak positions) as observed in the case of pristine ZnO film (a), ascertaining the presence of major ZnO host content and the substantial amount of N-CQDs [the nominal ratio (0.6 x10-3 for film b and 1.2 x10-3 for film c) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v))] in its coexistence in the hybrid thin films. The absorption band of CC stretching vibrations originated from the core of N-CQDs provides the finger print for the incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid thin films. Interestingly, the CC stretching vibrations centered at 2191 cm−1 along with the other weak absorption bands (2290 cm−1 assigned to CN, 2308 cm−1 to CO/CN, and 2372 cm−1 to CO stretching) (Fig. 5(iii)) showed the increased IR absorption intensity upon increasing N-CQDs content, ascertaining the incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid thin films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman studies of N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The Raman spectral measurements were carried out for the synthesized N-CQDs powder, pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) [the nominal ratio (0.6 x10-3 for film b and 1.2 x10-3 for film c) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v))] with the excitation wavelength of 633 nm (He-Ne laser) to explore the major Raman features (Fig. 6 ). Raman spectrum (Fig. 6(i)) of N-CQDs powder exhibits distinct characteristic Raman bands centered at 1576 cm−1 (G band -attributed to the in-plane vibrations of sp2 hybridized CC bonds originated from the core of hexagonal lattice structure) and 1348 cm−1 (D band - related to sp3 hybridized C atoms, functionalized disorders and defects) [61]. The peak intensity ratio of D band to G band (D/G: 1.55) reveals the presence of large amounts of defects and functional groups (disorders) at the edges or surface of the prepared N-CQDs [61].

The Fig. 6(ii - iii) show the Raman spectra of the pristine ZnO and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (a - c). The sol–gel derived spin-coated ZnO thin films commonly exhibit hexagonal wurtzite crystal structure belonging to the space group
C
6 ν
4
(P63 mc) [15,47,62,63]. The Raman spectrum of pristine ZnO film (a) reveals the existence of lattice vibrational modes as predicted on the basis of group theory [A1(TO) at 366 cm−1, A1(LO) at 550 cm−1, E1(TO) at 419 cm−1, E2(low) at 212 cm−1, and E2(high) at 433 cm−1] along with the peak positioned at 313 cm−1 corresponding to the second order Raman processes [15,47,62,63]. The presence of doublet E2 vibrational mode (non-polar mode: Infrared inactive and only Raman active) with two phonon frequencies, E2(low) at 212 cm−1 and E2(high) at 433 cm−1, ascertains the characteristic wurtzite phase of ZnO (film a). The observed phonon mode E2(high) (433 cm−1) and the longitudinal optical (LO) component A1(LO) at 550 cm−1 (The polar vibrational mode: both Infrared and Raman active) corroborate the preferred (002) plane c-axis orientation of ZnO film (a) [15,64]. The unassigned additional peaks may be ascribed to substrate and the residue formed from the precursor solution and during the handling of films under the normal ambient. From the Raman spectrum of hybrid thin film (b), it is observed that the intensity of the observed lattice vibration modes of wurtzite ZnO (film a) is significantly increased upon incorporation of N-CQDs with the nominal ratio (0.6 x10-3) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v)) in the hybrid film (b), showing the improved quality (size-dependent property) of ZnO crystallites. The increased intensity of the polar longitudinal optical (LO) phonon modes observed in the ZnO/Ag nanostructure has been related with the formation (bonding) of charge transfer complex/species on the surface of ZnO as reported elsewhere [63]. A slight increase in the frequency of E2(high) (433 cm−1) phonon vibration mode is ascribed to compressive stress within ZnO which may be attributed to adsorption of N-CQDs on the surface of ZnO crystallites in the film (b) [14,15]. In Fig. 6(iii), the observed Raman vibrational band centered at 1347 cm−1 (corresponding to d-band related to defects and/or functional disorders of N-CQDs) in the pristine ZnO film is slightly shifted to 1344 cm−1 with a small increase in the peak intensity in the case of hybrid thin film (b), ascertaining the presence of incorporated N-CQDs on the surface of the hybrid film (b).

Upon the further increase of N-CQDs content in the hybrid film (c) with the nominal ratio (1.2 x10-3) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v)), the intensity of observed lattice vibrational modes of ZnO is further increased without any significant change in the peak positions when compared to that of film (b), supporting the above results that the incorporated N-CQDs surround the ZnO particles and control (capping) its size in nanoscale which in turn facilitates the enhanced peak intensity. The consecutive increase in the Raman peak intensity of the vibrational mode at 1561 cm−1 corresponding to G-band related to sp2 hybridized CC bonds (core) of the N-CQDs in the N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) when compared to that of pristine film (a) essentially indicates the increasing content of incorporated N-CQDs in the hybrid thin films. The Raman band peak corresponding to d-band of N-CQDs is further shifted to 1341 cm−1 with slight reduction in the peak intensity for the increased N-CQDs content in the hybrid thin film (c) [Fig. 6(iii)], ascertaining the incorporated N-CQDs enter into the bulk of the hybrid film (c) and also make corresponding change in the coordination environment of various functional groups of N-CQDs onto the surface of ZnO nanoparticles [65]. The Raman studies of the pristine and hybrid thin films (a - c) essentially support to report that the incorporated N-CQDs may get adsorbed onto the surface of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO and control its size at nanoscale in accordance with the above results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS studies of N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed to probe the chemical composition of synthesized N-CQDs powder as shown in the Fig. 7 . The wide survey scan spectrum (Fig. 7(i)) shows three major binding energy (BE) peaks centered at 283.2 eV (corresponding to carbon; C 1 s core level), 398.4 eV (nitrogen: N 1 s), and 530.5 eV (oxygen: O 1 s), ascertaining the major constituent elements of the synthesized N-CQDs [51,66,67]. The obtained narrow scan C 1 s core level spectrum (Fig. 7(ii)) is deconvoluted by using Gaussian fitting, exhibiting the three types of carbon bonds (the dominant BE peak at 283.4 eV is assigned to CC bonds of sp2 graphitic structure, 284.9 eV to CC bonds, and 287.1 eV to CO/CO bonds) [51,66]. The deconvoluted O 1 s core level spectrum essentially reveals two major oxygen bonds [530.3 eV ascribed to physically adsorbed oxygen (CO bonds) on the surface of N-CQDs, and 532.09 eV to CO bonds], demonstrating the presence of various O containing functional groups including isolated –OH, -CO, and -O-CO (Fig. 7(iii)). The strong nitrogen (N 1 s) signal with the major deconvoluted BE peaks at 398.6 (CN), 399.9 (NH2), 400.6 (CNC), and 401.2 eV (N-C3) (Fig. 7(iv)), also indicates the effective doping of nitrogen atoms in the synthesized N-CQDs [51,66]. These results corroborate the hydrothermally prepared N-CQDs are having sp2 hybridized carbon core (graphitic) structure decorated by various O and N containing functional groups at the edges and/or on the surface. The XPS BE peaks (along with deconvoluted peaks) and the corresponding bonding’s of the main elements present in N-CQDs powder, pristine ZnO (a), and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) are given in the Table 2 .

The XPS spectra of the sol–gel derived spin-coated pristine ZnO (film a) and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) with the nominal ratio (0.6 x10-3 for film b and 1.2 x10-3 for film c) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v)), are shown in Fig. 8 . The full scan survey spectra (in the BE range of 0–1300 eV) of the films (a - c) exhibit the corresponding X-ray photoelectron BE peaks of the main constituent elements of ZnO and N-CQDs such as zinc (Zn), oxygen (O), and carbon (C) as shown in Fig. 8(i). The signal corresponding to the dopant nitrogen (N 1 s) atoms of the N-CQDs is not observed due to the non-traceable quantity of its coexistence in the N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films. The Zn 2p core level spectrum (Fig. 8(ii)) of the pristine ZnO thin film (a) reveals the two BE peaks (doublets) centered at 1019.2 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and 1042.4 eV (Zn 2p1/2) with the energy difference of 23 eV, indicating the existence of Zn atoms in the + 2 oxidization state (Zn2+ ions in Zn-O bonds) [47]. The asymmetric O 1 s core level spectrum (Fig. 8(iii)) of the film (a) is deconvoluted and having a dominant peak at 528.74 eV (OI) corresponding to the oxygen (O2–) ions surrounded by zinc (Zn2+) ions (Zn-O bonds) and a shoulder peak at 530.22 eV (OII) which is usually attributed to chemisorbed/dissociated oxygen or hydroxyl/carbonyl groups on the surface of the spin-coated pristine ZnO film [14,47,68]. The BE peak at 283.5 eV is assigned to adventitious carbon atoms (C 1 s core level electrons) formed during the coating and/or handling of the films under the normal ambient (Fig. 8(iv)) [15]. The observed C 1 s BE peak value (283.5 eV) of the pristine ZnO film (a) is used as charge reference for the charge correction (energy-scale calibration). The core level spectra (Zn 2p, O 1 s, and C 1 s) of the respective N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin film are calibrated (energy-scale corrected) by the observed BE peak shift of C 1 s core level spectrum of the same hybrid thin film to be aligned with the charge reference BE value of 283.5 eV.

The BE peaks of Zn 2p doublets [Zn 2p3/2 (1018.9 eV) and Zn 2p1/2(1041.9 eV)] of the hybrid thin film (b) with the ratio (0.6 x10-3) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v)) exhibit the slight BE peak shift towards lower energy with significant decrease in the peaks' intensity when compared to that of pristine film (a), attributed to the shielding effect of valance electrons in Zn ions in the nanocrystalline ZnO for the improved electrical properties [69]. The asymmetric O 1 s core level spectrum [having the deconvoluted peaks at 528.52 eV (OI) and 530.02 eV (OII)] of the film (b) also showed the decreased intensity with a small shift in the BE peak positions on the lower BE side. The intensity of charge corrected C 1 s BE peak (283.5 eV) is drastically increased when compared to that of pristine film (a), ascertaining the incorporation of the N-CQDs in the hybrid thin film (b). The significant change in the binding energies of Zn and O ions in the Zn-O bonds with considerable decrease in their intensity and along with the drastic increase in the intensity (as a measure of incorporated N-CQDs) of C 1 s binding energy peak corroborate the presence of incorporated N-CQDs on the surface of the hybrid thin film (b).

The N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin film (c) with the nominal ratio (1.2 x10-3) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v)) exhibited the BE peak shift (0.2 eV) of Zn 2p doublets [Zn 2p3/2 (1019.1 eV) and Zn 2p1/2 (1042.1 eV)] to the higher binding energy side when compared to that [Zn 2p3/2 (1018.9 eV) and Zn 2p1/2 (1041.9 eV)] of the hybrid thin film (b). The BE peak intensity of doublet Zn 2p (film c) is significantly increased (when compared to film b) which is slightly higher than that of pristine ZnO (film a). The asymmetric BE peak intensity of O 1 s core level spectrum of the film (c) having the deconvoluted dominant BE peak at 528.62 eV (OI) and shoulder peak at 530.02 eV (OII) is increased along with the slight shift in the BE peak position when compared to that of film (b). It is also observed that the BE peak intensity of C 1 s core level spectrum is drastically decreased when compared to that of film (b). The increased BE peak intensity of Zn 2p and O 1 s (OI) states (corresponding to Zn-O bonds) and the decreased BE peak intensity of C 1 s state (related to N-CQDs) ascertain that the incorporated N-CQDs are entered into the bulk of the N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin film (c). The existence of chemical interaction (via hydrogen bonding or non-covalent bonding, van der Walls forces) (chemisorption) and/or charge transfer groups between the anchored organic ligand/molecules (tris-(8-hydroxyquinoline)aluminum (Alq3)) and inorganic surface functionalized ZnO in the spin-coated Alq3/ZnO hybrid thin films has been studied as reported elsewhere [14,15]. In addition, the formation of strong bonding (via hydrogen or non-covalent bond) by the incorporated organic ligand molecules onto the mesoporous silica has been reported in preparation of the organic/mesoporous silica composite material for the detection of trace amount of toxic copper(II) ions in the contaminated waste water [70]. Awual et al. has also reported anchoring of hydroxyl groups abundant on the surface of silica with organic ligand heteroatoms by non-covalent affinity (hydrogen bonding) in the mesoporous silica/organic composite material which is prepared to detect nitrite(NO2 −) ions in the waste water management process [71]. In this study, the observed change in the BE peak positions and intensity of Zn 2p, O 1 s (OI) (corresponding to Zn-O bonds), and C 1 s (related to incorporated N-CQDs) core level spectra of N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films corroborates that the incorporated N-CQDs may have anchored (existence of chemical interaction via non-covalent bonding (chemisorption) and/or charge transfer complex) onto the surface of ZnO and facilitate the size-control of ZnO (by capping) at nanoscale in the solution-processed hybrid thin films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence studies of N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the synthesized N-CQDs powder, pristine ZnO (film a), and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films (b and c) are shown in the Fig. 9 . Fig. 9(i) exhibits the PL emission spectra of N-CQDs solution (powder dispersed in water) at different excitation wavelengths (λex: 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, and 420 nm). Upon exciting the solution at 320 nm, it showed the PL emission peak (λem) at 434 nm. It is also observed that the PL emission peak intensity is increasing along with red-shift in the peak wavelengths [442 (λex: 340 nm), 450 (360 nm), 455 (380 nm), 458 (400 nm), and 477 nm (420 nm)] (Fig. 9(ii)) and the highest intensity is observed at 455 nm (λex: 380 nm), which is a typical characteristics of carbon nanodots [72,73]. The observed excitation dependent PL characteristics can be attributed to formation of different energy levels (arising from emissive traps, excitons of carbon, quantum confinement effect, oxygen-containing functional groups, sp2 hybridized core structure, and edge disorders/defects) and efficient radiative recombination pathways in the N-CQDs [72,74].

Fig. 9(iii) exhibits the characteristic PL emission spectrum of the pristine ZnO thin film (a) at the excitation wavelength (λex) of 325 nm, showing a strong band-edge excitonic emission around 383 nm (UV) along with a broad defect-related emission covering the visible region centered at around 537 nm [14,75]. The PL emission in the visible region is covered by various defects formed in the prepared ZnO films such as (i) oxygen vacancies or oxygen interstitials in the green-yellow region due to the recombination of delocalized electrons near the conduction band with deeply trapped holes in singly ionized oxygen vacancies and (ii) the oxygen vacancy-related blue-green emission band, which is ascribed to the radiative energy transfer from the energy levels in the ZnO energy gap [14,75,76]. Upon the incorporation of N-CQDs with the nominal ratio (0.6 x10-3) of N-CQDs/ZnO (%(w/v)) in the hybrid thin film (b), the characteristic band-edge emission (centered at around 387 nm) is drastically increased and the defect-related visible emission is significantly decreased for the same excitation (λex) of 325 nm in the PL spectrum of hybrid film (b) as shown in Fig. 9(iii). The considerable broadening of band-edge emission in the PL emission spectrum of hybrid film (b) on the higher wavelength side with the FWHM of 43 nm when compared to that (25 nm) of pristine ZnO film (a) also ascertains the incorporation of N-CQDs in the hybrid thin film and its contribution to the PL emission. In our earlier study, it has been reported that the organic Alq3 molecules in the sol–gel derived spin-coated Alq3/ZnO hybrid thin films absorb the incident UV energy and non-radiatively transfer its energy via luminescent quencher [carbonyl group (CO)] to ZnO, resulting in a four-fold enhancement in the band-edge emission (∼380 nm) of ZnO [14]. Similarly, from the optical studies of Alq3 thin films, it has also been reported that the carbonyl group (CO) formed in one of the three 8-hydroxyquinoline (HQ) units of the Alq3 molecule during the light exposure under the normal ambient is acting as a luminescent quencher which is trapping the charge carriers and non-radiatively transferring its energy in the film [77]. The recent report has demonstrated that the coating of CQDs (obtained by a novel method of rapid thermal heating process of spin-coated poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) film) around the vertically aligned ZnO NRs induces CO and Zn-C bonds at the CQDs/ZnO NR interface which facilitate the charge transfer between them and result in the enhanced PL emission of ZnO (both in UV and visible emissions) and efficient photo sensing of CQDs/ZnO NR core/shell structure in the device [35]. In this study, the increased intensity of band-edge emission (387 nm) of hybrid film (b) is attributed to the fact that upon excitation of 325 nm, the incorporated N-CQDs observe the incident energy and efficiently transfer it non-radiatively to ZnO (via existing luminescent quenchers and/or charge transfer complex species formed at the N-CQDs/ZnO interface), resulting in the enhanced band-edge emission, along with its contribution to the characteristic visible emission. The significant decrease in the defect-related visible emission of hybrid film (b) when compared to that of pristine ZnO (film a) is attributed to the improved quality of ZnO due to the incorporated N-CQDs get adsorbed onto the surface of ZnO crystallites and control its size at nanoscale which in turn reduces the defects density and improves the crystalline quality of ZnO particles.

The hydrothermal synthesized N-CQDs reveal the narrow size distribution in the range of 2.5 to 3 nm and exhibit the fluorescent emission in the visible region through various radiative recombination pathways upon UV excitation. The spin-coated N-CQDs incorporated ZnO (N-CQDs/ZnO) hybrid thin films have the hexagonal wurtzite structure with higher transparency in the visible region (average transmittance of above 81 %). The incorporated N-CQDs get adsorbed onto the surface of the ZnO crystallites and control its size in nanoscale, ascertaining the existence of chemical interaction (chemisorption) and/or charge transfer groups between N-CQDs and ZnO nanoparticles in the hybrid thin films. The band-edge emission (383 nm) of ZnO increases for the increasing concentration of N-CQDs, showing the non-radiative energy transfer from N-CQD to ZnO in the hybrid thin films. The significant broadening of band-edge emission towards the higher wavelengths with slight red-shift in the peak wavelength corroborates the fluorescent emission contribution of N-CQDs in the hybrid thin film when excited at 325 nm (UV energy).
